agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (stock) with Sales/Order Management (orders) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This fundamentally changes the relationship between two currently decoupled bounded contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The request threatens to collapse two distinct bounded contexts into a single coupled system.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer >= 0 representing the current quantity of a Product available in the warehouse. Owned by Context A (Warehouse Management)."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase one or more products, composed of OrderLines. Owned by Context B (Sales/Order Management)."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A single line item within an Order, referencing a Product by ID and a desired Quantity."},{"term":"Product","definition":"An item that exists in both contexts but with different meaning — in Warehouse it is a stockable unit, in Sales it is a purchasable item. These are NOT the same entity."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A temporary hold on stock quantity to fulfill a pending order. Does NOT currently exist — would need to be introduced as a new domain concept if this feature ships."},{"term":"Stock Availability Projection","definition":"A read-only, eventually-consistent view of stock levels exposed to the Sales context. Must NOT be the authoritative StockLevel — it is a projection, not the source of truth."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel in Context A must never go below 0.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A negative stock level represents a physically impossible state — you cannot pick items that do not exist. Violating this corrupts warehouse data integrity.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: After any operation, Product.StockLevel >= 0 in Context A."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Context A (Warehouse) is the sole authority on stock truth. Context B (Sales) must NEVER write to or directly mutate StockLevel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If two contexts can mutate the same aggregate, you lose consistency guarantees. Warehouse owns stock; this is a fundamental bounded context boundary.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: No command originating from the Sales context directly modifies StockLevel in the Warehouse context."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"An Order placement in Context B must not synchronously depend on Context A's availability. The check must be asynchronous or use a projected read model.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an architectural policy to preserve bounded context autonomy. A VP could decide to accept tighter coupling, but it would be a deliberate trade-off.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: If Context A is temporarily unavailable, Context B can still accept orders (possibly in a pending/unconfirmed state) rather than hard-failing."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Stock availability shown to the sales team is eventually consistent — it is a projection, not a guarantee. It must never be presented as a real-time guarantee of fulfillable stock.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Network latency, concurrent orders, and warehouse operations mean any cross-context read is stale by definition. This is a policy choice about how to communicate uncertainty.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: The stock availability indicator includes a staleness signal (e.g., 'as of X seconds ago') or is clearly labeled as approximate."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Between the time stock is checked and the time stock is actually picked, the quantity may change. A Reservation concept is required to bridge this gap.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without reservations, two concurrent orders for the last item could both pass the availability check but only one can be fulfilled. This is a classic TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) race condition that violates BR-1.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: When an order is accepted as fulfillable, a corresponding StockReservation is created in Context A that decrements available (not physical) stock."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Stock Reservations must have a time-to-live (TTL). Expired reservations release stock back to the available pool.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The TTL duration is a business policy (e.g., 15 minutes, 24 hours). But the existence of expiry prevents stock from being permanently locked by abandoned orders.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: A reservation older than its TTL is automatically released and the reserved quantity is added back to available stock."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer places an order for Product X with Quantity 5","when":"The Warehouse context reports available stock for Product X >= 5","then":"The order is accepted and a stock reservation for 5 units of Product X is created in the Warehouse context"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer places an order for Product X with Quantity 5","when":"The Warehouse context reports available stock for Product X < 5","then":"The order is blocked/rejected with a clear reason indicating insufficient stock, and no reservation is created"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Two customers simultaneously place orders for the last 3 units of Product X (each ordering 3)","when":"Both orders are processed","then":"Exactly one order is accepted and one is blocked. StockLevel never goes below 0. No oversell occurs."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The Warehouse Management system is temporarily unavailable","when":"A customer places an order","then":"The order is accepted in a 'pending confirmation' state rather than hard-rejected. The system retries stock verification when Context A recovers."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A stock reservation was created 'TTL' minutes ago and the order was never confirmed/paid","when":"The reservation TTL expires","then":"The reservation is released and the reserved quantity is returned to available stock"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The sales team is viewing the order management dashboard","when":"Stock levels change in the Warehouse context","then":"The stock availability indicator updates within an acceptable delay (seconds, not minutes) and is clearly marked as approximate"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An order with multiple OrderLines is placed","when":"Stock is sufficient for some lines but not all","then":"The entire order is blocked (atomic check) — partial fulfillment is a separate business decision that must be explicitly designed, not accidentally allowed"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Concurrent orders exhaust stock between check and reservation","expected_behavior":"The reservation mechanism in Context A must be the serialization point. Only the first reservation to claim the last units succeeds. The second order receives an insufficient stock response."},{"scenario":"Stock received from supplier while orders are queued as blocked","expected_behavior":"Blocked orders are NOT automatically retried unless an explicit re-evaluation workflow exists. Stock receipt is a Warehouse event; Sales must subscribe to decide whether to re-check blocked orders."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Sales but not yet in Warehouse","expected_behavior":"Order should be blocked with a clear 'product not stocked' distinction from 'product out of stock'. These are different domain situations."},{"scenario":"Order is placed for quantity 0","expected_behavior":"Rejected at the Sales context level — this is a validation concern, not a stock concern. Should never reach Context A."},{"scenario":"Stock availability indicator shows 'available' but by the time order is submitted, stock is gone","expected_behavior":"This is expected and acceptable. The indicator is a hint, not a guarantee. The order is blocked at submission time via the reservation mechanism. UI should handle this gracefully."},{"scenario":"Reservation created but payment fails or order is cancelled","expected_behavior":"Reservation must be explicitly released. If the system crashes between order cancellation and reservation release, the TTL ensures eventual release."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on insufficient stock prevents overselling, which eliminates costly backorder handling, customer refund processing, and expedited shipping for late fulfillment. However, false rejections due to stale stock data could cause lost revenue.","operational":"Introduces cross-context coupling that increases deployment complexity. Both contexts must now be versioned and deployed with awareness of each other. The reservation mechanism adds operational overhead (monitoring TTLs, handling orphaned reservations). Real-time stock projection requires an event pipeline.","user":"Sales team gains visibility into stock, reducing manual checks and phone calls to the warehouse. Customers get faster feedback on order feasibility. However, 'approximate' stock indicators may confuse users who expect real-time accuracy.","risk":"HIGH — This integration is the single most common source of distributed system bugs in e-commerce. TOCTOU race conditions, eventual consistency confusion, and cascading failures between contexts are all likely. Additionally, if the 'blocking' logic is implemented in Context B using Context A data, it creates a hidden coupling that will break when either context evolves independently.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Orders may be rejected that previously would have been accepted and fulfilled. Customer experience depends heavily on how 'blocked' orders are communicated."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains stock visibility (positive) but must understand the indicator is approximate. May over-rely on it and blame the system when orders are blocked despite showing 'available'."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Now implicitly coupled to order flow via reservations. Reservation TTL expiry and stock availability events add new operational responsibilities."},{"group":"Engineering/Platform Team","impact":"Must build and maintain an event-driven integration layer, stock projection read model, and reservation lifecycle management. Significant new infrastructure."},{"group":"Finance/Accounting","impact":"Reservation-based stock holding affects inventory valuation and reporting. Reserved stock is neither sold nor fully available — accounting treatment must be defined."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The feature request is ambiguous in critical areas: it says 'automatically block' but doesn't define the order lifecycle for blocked orders (retry? cancel? notify?). It says 'real-time' stock indicator but doesn't acknowledge eventual consistency constraints. It doesn't mention reservations, which are essential to correctness. The spec conflates two bounded contexts without addressing integration patterns. Multiple missing business decisions lower confidence.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 invariant is clear and non-negotiable","Context A owns stock truth — this is fundamental DDD","Concurrent order race conditions require a reservation mechanism","The stock indicator cannot be truly real-time across bounded contexts"],"low_confidence_areas":["What happens to 'blocked' orders — are they rejected, queued, or held for retry?","Partial fulfillment policy — should multi-line orders be all-or-nothing?","Reservation TTL duration — business policy not specified","Whether Context B should degrade gracefully or hard-fail when Context A is down","How 'real-time' the stock indicator actually needs to be"],"assumptions":["The two bounded contexts should remain separate (not merged)","Stock reservations are the correct pattern to bridge the TOCTOU gap","The stock indicator is a read-model projection, not a direct query to Context A","Blocked orders mean rejected at placement time, not queued for later fulfillment"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The feature request says 'automatically block' orders but does not define the lifecycle of a blocked order. Is it permanently rejected? Held in a pending state? Queued for automatic retry when stock arrives? Each interpretation has vastly different domain, UX, and technical implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["A) Reject the order immediately with a clear error — customer must retry manually","B) Accept the order in a 'pending stock' state and automatically fulfill when stock arrives","C) Reject but notify the customer when stock becomes available"],"recommendation":"Option A for MVP — it is the simplest to implement correctly and avoids the complexity of order lifecycle state machines. Options B and C can be added later as explicit features."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real-time stock available indicator' contradicts the reality of eventually consistent cross-context data. The spec needs to define acceptable staleness and how approximation is communicated to users.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["A) Near-real-time via event streaming (seconds of delay, labeled as approximate)","B) Polling-based refresh (e.g., every 30 seconds)","C) On-demand refresh button with cached default"],"recommendation":"Option A with clear UX labeling that the indicator is approximate. Never use the word 'real-time' in the UI."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No policy defined for multi-line orders where stock is available for some lines but not others. Atomic (all-or-nothing) vs. partial fulfillment is a critical business decision that changes the entire domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Team","Finance/Accounting"],"options":["A) All-or-nothing: block entire order if any line has insufficient stock","B) Partial fulfillment: accept available lines, block or backorder the rest","C) Customer choice: present options and let the customer decide"],"recommendation":"Option A as default — partial fulfillment introduces split shipments, partial payments, and significant accounting complexity. Should be a deliberate Phase 2 decision."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Direct synchronous coupling between Sales and Warehouse contexts","business_rule_broken":"BR-2, BR-3 — If implemented as a synchronous call from Context B to Context A during order placement, it violates bounded context autonomy and creates a single point of failure."},{"violation":"Stock check without reservation mechanism","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-5 — Any implementation that checks stock availability without creating a reservation will inevitably allow overselling under concurrent load, violating the StockLevel >= 0 invariant."},{"violation":"Presenting stock projection as guaranteed truth","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 — If the stock indicator in the Sales UI is presented as exact/real-time without acknowledging eventual consistency, it will mislead the sales team and generate support escalations when orders are blocked despite showing 'available'."}]}

This fundamentally changes the relationship between two currently decoupled bounded contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management.
